g.data-narration: Welcome to Transaction Support in SQL. We explore how databases ensure reliable and consistent data operations.
Transactions are essential when multiple operations must succeed together or fail together to maintain data integrity.
g.text: Consider a bank transfer: debiting one account and crediting another must happen together.
If only one operation succeeds, data becomes inconsistent and money can be lost.

g.data-narration: A transaction is a logical unit of work containing one or more SQL operations that execute as a single atomic unit.
g.text: A transaction is a logical unit of work consisting of one or more SQL statements that
execute as a single atomic unit. Either ALL operations succeed or NONE are applied.

g.data-narration: ACID properties ensure reliable transaction processing: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability.
A transaction moves through states: Active, Partially Committed, Committed, Failed, and Aborted during its lifecycle.
COMMIT permanently saves all changes made during the current transaction, releasing locks and ending the transaction.
g.text: Permanently saves all changes made in the transaction to the database.
Releases all locks and makes changes visible to other transactions.

g.data-narration: ROLLBACK undoes all uncommitted changes, restoring the database to its state before the transaction began.
g.text: Undoes all changes made since the transaction began or since the last savepoint.
Restores database to its original consistent state. Use when errors occur.

g.data-narration: SAVEPOINT creates intermediate checkpoints within a transaction, enabling partial rollback without losing the entire transaction.
g.text: Creates a named checkpoint within a transaction. Allows rolling back to that point
without losing earlier operations in the same transaction.
BEGIN → SAVEPOINT → INSERT → ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT

g.data-narration: Concurrent transactions can cause problems: dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and phantom reads that violate data consistency.
Four isolation levels control transaction visibility: READ UNCOMMITTED, READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, and SERIALIZABLE.
A bank transfer demonstrates transactions: debit from sender, credit to receiver, with COMMIT on success or ROLLBACK on failure.
In summary, transactions ensure ACID properties through COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT, and appropriate isolation levels.
g.text: COMMIT saves changes, ROLLBACK undoes them
Higher isolation = More protection, Less concurrency
Most databases default to READ COMMITTED
